---
subtitle: "VectorByte Methods Training"
title: "Introduction to Traits"
author: "The VectorByte Team (Cat Lippi, PhD, Virginia Tech)"
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: graphics/VectorByte-logo_lg.png
  data-background-size: contain
  data-background-opacity: "0.2"
format: revealjs
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(cache = FALSE, 
                      echo = FALSE, 
                      message = FALSE, 
                      warning = FALSE,
                      #fig.height=6, 
                      #fig.width = 1.777777*6,
                      tidy = FALSE, 
                      comment = NA, 
                      highlight = TRUE, 
                      prompt = FALSE, 
                      crop = TRUE,
                      comment = "#>",
                      collapse = TRUE)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(xtable)
library(viridis)

options(stringsAsFactors=FALSE)
knit_hooks$set(no.main = function(before, options, envir) {
    if (before) par(mar = c(4.1, 4.1, 1.1, 1.1))  # smaller margin on top
})
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
knitr::opts_knit$set(width = 60)
source("my_knitter.R")
#library(tidyverse)
#library(reshape2)
#theme_set(theme_light(base_size = 16))
make_latex_decorator <- function(output, otherwise) {
  function() {
      if (knitr:::is_latex_output()) output else otherwise
  }
}
insert_pause <- make_latex_decorator(". . .", "\n")
insert_slide_break <- make_latex_decorator("----", "\n")
insert_inc_bullet <- make_latex_decorator("> *", "*")
insert_html_math <- make_latex_decorator("", "$$")
## classoption: aspectratio=169
```

## Introduction to Traits - Objectives

• Define a “trait”

• Understand the role of traits (and their variation) in VBDs

• Discuss how traits can be incorporated into VBD predictions

## What is a vector?

Any agent which carries and transmits an infectious agent between hosts

<center>(graphics/intro_traits_1.jpg)</center>

## What kinds of animals are vectors?

Many! Although arthropods are most commonly studied

<center>(graphics/intro_traits_2.jpg)</center>

## Overview of VBD Transmission

Mechanical Transmission

(graphics/intro_traits_3a.jpg)(graphics/intro_traits_3b.jpg)

## Overview of VBD Transmission

Biological Transmission

(graphics/intro_traits_4a.jpg)(graphics/intro_traits_4b.jpg)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

<center>(graphics/intro_traits_5.jpg)</center>

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
Pathogens undergo obligate develoment in the mosquito 
<center>(graphics/intro_traits_7.jpg)</center>

## VBD Transmission is the Instersection of Two Cycles 
Intrinsic and Extrinsic Incubation Periods 
<center>(graphics/intro_traits_8.jpg)</center>

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

<center>(graphics/intro_traits_9.jpg)</center>

What elements do we need to include?

## What is a Trait?

Any measurable feature of an individual organism

Functional trait feeding rate, size, metabolic rate, eggs per day

Behavioral trait questing, host/food preference, thermal regulation, site selection

## Variability in Traits

<center>(graphics/intro_traits_13.jpg)</center>                       
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
We can get more complex/realistic 

<center>(graphics/SIR_2.jpg)</center>

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

But with every parameter added, there is a greater need for data

<center>(graphics/SIR_3.jpg)</center>
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

<center>(graphics/SIR_4.jpg)</center>

## R0 for a Directly-Transmitted Pathogen

<center>(graphics/intro_traits_10.jpg)</center>

## R0 for a Vector-Borne Pathogen

<center>(graphics/intro_traits_12.jpg)</center>

## Thermal Performance Curves (TPCs)

- Vectors of interest are typically ectothermic

- Biological reactions typically go faster as temperature increases

<center>(graphics/TPC_1.jpg)</center>

##But there's a limit!

<center>(graphics/TPC_3.jpg)</center>


-----------------------------------------------------------------------

- Nearly every aspect of life history is influenced by temperature

- We want to include parameters in relation to temperature

- Biologically meaningful models

- Old assumptions were linear        

- Unimodal curves are more realistic 

<center>(graphics/TPC_2.jpg)</center>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Different traits have different curves

<center>(graphics/TPC_6.jpg)</center>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Thermal limits are useful for predictions and mapping

## Estimating TPCs

- TPCs derived from empirical data from laboratory experiments

<center>(graphics/TPC_4.jpg)</center>

## Need to Collect Data Across a Thermal Gradient

- Ideally want to capture data across a wide thermal gradient
- Need enough temperatures to estimate minimum, maximum, and optimum values

## Fitting TPCs

- Thermal responses can have many possible shapes

<center>(graphics/TPC_7.jpg)</center>

## Model Validation

- Real data from the field or other experiments can help validate our estimates

<center>(graphics/TPC_5.jpg)</center>
